critique.summary: The article discusses the idea that one's identity should not be solely tied to their job or professional skills, especially in the context of automation and AI replacing certain technical roles. It emphasizes the importance of human relationships, presence, and empathy as irreplaceable aspects of personal value beyond occupational roles.
mainPoints: Identifying oneself solely by their job title is a constructed narrative, not an absolute truth.
Automation and AI are replacing many technical skills, but this does not diminish a person's inherent value.
Human qualities like empathy, presence, and genuine relationships cannot be automated and are central to personal worth.
Economic and social structures influence how individuals fare during technological transitions, not the technology itself.
End-of-life reflections show people regret neglecting relationships more than career achievements or money.
The article encourages readers to separate their self-worth from their job and focus on authentic human connections.
critique.truthfulness: The article presents a philosophical and psychological perspective rather than factual claims requiring verification. The references to Susan Fiske's research on warmth and competence are accurate and well-established in social psychology. The discussion about automation replacing certain jobs aligns with widely accepted economic and technological trends. The mention of Bronnie Ware's end-of-life regrets is consistent with her published work. Overall, the article's claims are thoughtful reflections supported by existing research and observable societal trends, though some points are subjective interpretations rather than empirical facts.
considerations: The article does not address the economic and social challenges faced by individuals displaced by automation in detail, such as unemployment or retraining needs.
It assumes a level of social support and governance that may not exist universally, which affects how people experience job loss.
The psychological impact of job loss can be complex and may require professional support beyond personal reflection.
The article focuses on individual identity and relationships but does not explore systemic solutions to labor displacement.
Cultural differences in how people relate to their work and identity are not discussed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the idea that individuals are more than their jobs, but acknowledges the practical and emotional challenges of job loss, economic dependency, and societal structures. Commenters debate the balance between personal identity, economic realities, and the evolving role of work in life.

comments.author: musicale
comments.text:
> You are not your job. You're a person first. Your ability to connect, be present, and make people feel understood is what makes you irreplaceable to the people around you, which is the only market that counts.

True, but losing your job is still a big deal. It often means that you lose your income, your health insurance (in the US at least), many (if not most) of your daily interactions with other people, and your social status.

> My technical skills are being disrupted by machines - that's fine I'll go do other things.

As others have noted, it's great to not actually need the paycheck you are working for.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the real-world consequences of job loss beyond identity, emphasizing financial and social impacts, which grounds the discussion in practical reality.

comments.author: weatherlite
comments.text:
Some people indeed identify too much with their jobs, but for many others getting replaced by A.I means on very practical terms - a huge hit in salary, it means possibly retraining - maybe for years, means stress to the family (mortgage, bills etc) perhaps even stress to the marriage. 
I disagree that the people near you only love you or need you for your presence; they also rely on your paycheck. Your daugher may love you for you but she needs that check to the private school, that money for nice clothes and gadgets like her friends all have and paying for that apartment in the nice neighborhood.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced view of the economic and emotional stress caused by job displacement, reinforcing that identity and financial support are intertwined.

comments.author: oytis
comments.text:
People don't need self-help advise, they need a fair redistribution of increased productivity.

We don't make a big deal of our jobs because we are stupid - it's the society that assigns this or that income to this or that job, and income determines lifestyle or in worst case the survival.
comments.analysis: The comment insightfully points out systemic issues around income distribution and societal valuation of work, challenging simplistic self-help narratives.

comments.author: david-gpu
comments.text:
Agreed with the title and some of the broad sentiment, but two things stood out.

> I can't delegate my capacity to sit with someone when they're confused or scared or just need to feel known

Plenty of people rely on therapists and/or chat bots to listen to them. Not everybody feels comfortable burdening their friends and family with their problems.

> We possess the means to care for everyone -- yet choose not to

There is a trade-off between social services in a broad sense and the ability to pay for them. The stronger the social safety net, the more people at the margin will choose to work less, earn less, make less of an effort. In turn, the tax base becomes smaller, and thus unable to maintain those same social services.

For example, the vast majority of people choose to retire once they reach the age where they are able to collect enough from their pension that they no longer need to work in order to get by. If we lowered the age of eligibility by a year, most people would retire a year earlier. Just like we see people retiring later in countries that have moved the eligibility to the age of e.g. 67.

With this I am not advocating to increase or decrease the current social safety net in whichever region you, dear reader, are living. I am simply pointing out some of the real-world effects of moving the needle in one direction or another.

Thus, yes, in rich countries we have collectively decided that "caring for everyone" is not the best way forward, because we see that it becomes unsustainable when you go too far. Where exactly we place the needle varies from place to place, obviously. Thinning the social safety net too far also has massive societal and economic consequences.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a balanced perspective on social safety nets, acknowledging both their benefits and economic trade-offs, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: ilamont
comments.text:
When you meet someone, you assess them on two dimensions. The first is warmth - do you believe they mean you well? The second is competence - do you believe they're capable?

Well, sometimes.

At other times, the assessment may be based on signalling, tribalism, perception of status, personal connections, career connections, transactional goals, or other criteria.

Some people don't have or can't show warmth. Or they don't have the ability to "crack a joke at the right time" or make small talk. Should that be held against people when making assessments?
comments.analysis: This comment critically examines social perception, highlighting complexity beyond simple warmth and competence metrics, which enriches the conversation.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
>My technical skills are being disrupted by machines - that's fine I'll go do other things. [links to long bike trip]

Ok that's cool and all but many of us have bills to pay. Bike trips don't pay the bills. Software people have been economically advantaged up until now that they can go and do stuff like that.
comments.analysis: The comment realistically challenges the idea that one can easily shift away from work, emphasizing economic constraints faced by most people.

comments.author: tomekw
comments.text:
I used to BE a software engineer. Then, I experienced a 3 years long burnout and got professional help. Now I work AS an Engineering Manager.

You are not your job. Do not put your ego in what you do. That’s something I discuss a lot during my 1:1s.
comments.analysis: This comment reinforces the article's main message with personal experience, advocating for separating identity from professional roles.

comments.author: beastman82
comments.text:
I want to thank everyone who hates work, is mentally checked out of their jobs and quiet quitting etc.

It makes it much easier for me to distinguish myself as a hard worker who cares about the business being successful. It also helps me keep my job during layoffs because I can assure you the managers have noticed.

When you are old and have lots of formative experiences that are not work-based, we can shake hands and mutually appreciate each other's motives and respective outcomes.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration and a competitive attitude toward disengaged coworkers, which adds emotional tension but less constructive insight.

comments.author: rmoriz
comments.text: I also took the route of finding a new hobby (biking, all things bike mechanics, even politics) but of course it's not paying any of my bills. That's the point. While I helped making other people very rich, I never owned shares or got a bonus after an exit.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights the economic realities of labor without equity, illustrating the gap between personal fulfillment and financial security.

comments.author: ashwinnair99
comments.text:
The people who figure this out early are rare. 
Most only get there after losing the job or burning out completely. 
Shame it takes that long
comments.analysis: This comment offers a realistic observation about the difficulty of detaching identity from work until forced by circumstances.
